---
title: "EVOLVE — Computer Vision in Extreme Live Music Environments"
subtitle: "Detection of Scene Components under Low-Light and High-Motion Conditions"
authors:
  - ACVial38
  - rsquaredata
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

# 1. Project Overview

EVOLVE is a computer vision project focusing on **object detection** in images captured during live metal concerts.

These environments combine several challenging visual conditions:

-   very low illumination
-   strong colored lighting (red, blue)
-   motion blur
-   dense crowds
-   handheld camera instability


Such conditions are rarely represented in standard computer vision benchmarks (e.g. COCO, Pascal VOC), yet they are common in real-world applications.

The objective of this project is to **fine-tune an existing deep convolutional neural network** to detect key visual components of concert scenes under these extreme conditions.

# 2. Task Definition

## 2.1. Chosen Task

> Object detection of semantic scene components in extreme live metal concert environments

The task consists in detecting and localizing predefined visual elements using bounding boxes.

A transfer learning approach is adopted, starting from a model pre-trained on a large-scale dataset and fine-tuned on a custom annotated dataset.

## 2.2. Target Classes

The following six classes are defined:

| **Class name** | **Definition** | **Annotation rules** |
|---------------|----------------|----------------------|
| `amp` | Stage amplifier or stage monitor visible on stage (e.g. guitar amp, bass amp, wedge monitor) | Rectangular or cubic object placed on the ground or stacked. One bounding box per amplifier or grouped boxes allowed if multiple amps are tightly packed. |
| `guitar` | Guitar or bass instrument visible in the image | Instrument must be visually identifiable (body and/or neck). The musician is not the target, only the instrument. |
| `drums` | Drum kit or part of a drum kit (snare, toms, cymbals, bass drum) | Partial visibility is sufficient. The drummer’s body is not required. |
| `micro` | Vocal microphone, handheld or on a stand | Microphone must be clearly identifiable. Hand + microphone is acceptable. The singer as a person is ignored. |
| `mosh_pit` | Area of intense collective crowd movement (pogo, circle pit, wall of death) | Annotate the region, not individuals. One large bounding box is sufficient. |
| `hands_raised` | Area containing multiple raised hands or arms in the crowd | At least two arms or hands must be visible simultaneously. Group-level annotation only. |

### Class Design Rationale

All classes correspond to concrete, visually observable objects or structured crowd patterns, ensuring annotation consistency and suitability for object detection models.

## 2.3. Ultra-Precise Annotation Protocol

### 2.3.1. General Rules (apply to all classes)

**Image-level rules**:
-   Annotate only what is clearly visible
-   If unsure → do not annotate
-   One image can contain multiple classes
-   Overlapping bounding boxes are allowed
-   Bounding boxes may be coarse (pixel-perfect precision is NOT required)

❌ What NOT to do:
-   Do not guess hidden objects
-   Do not annotate extremely small or barely visible elements
-   Do not annotate reflections, posters, or screens

### 2.3.2. Class Specific Annotation Rules

`amp`:
-   Annotate each visible amplifier OR group them if tightly packed
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   speaker arrays hanging above the stage
    -   PA systems far from the stage
-   If unsure between amp and generic box → ignore

`guitar`:
-   Annotate when:
    -   guitar or bass body or neck is visible
-   Partial visibility is OK (e.g. neck only)
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   cables
    -   guitar cases
    -   blurred shapes with no clear guitar form

`drums`:
-   Annotate when:
    -   at least one drum element is clearly identifiable
-   Cymbals alone are sufficient
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   decorative drum graphics on screens
    -   crowd members holding round objects

`micro`:
-   Annotate when:
    -   microphone shape is clearly visible
    -   handheld or on a stand
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   headset microphones
    -   distant mic stands without visible microphone head
-   Priority rule:
    -   if a microphone is visible → annotate `micro` **even if the singer is moving**

`mosh_pit`:
-   Annotate when:
    -   collective chaotic movement is visible
    -   structure such as circle pit, pogo, wall of death can be inferred visually
-   Use one large bounding box
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   static crowd
    -   small local movements

`hands_raised`:
-   Annotate when:
    -   **two or more arms/hands** are raised simultaneously
    -   gesture is collective and visible
-   Do NOT annotate:
    -   single raised arm
    -   musician’s arm on stage
-   One box per group is sufficient

### 2.3.3. Conflict Resolution Rules

When two classes could apply:

| **Situation**                   | **Rule**                                         |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Guitarist singing into a mic    | Annotate **`micro`** and **`guitar`** separately |
| Drum kit partially hidden       | Annotate **`drums`** if any part is visible      |
| Raised hands inside a mosh pit  | Annotate **both** `mosh_pit` and `hands_raised`  |
| Static crowd with raised phones | Ignore (no class)                                |


### 2.3.4. Dataset Balance Targets

Aim for **at least**:
-   30–40 instances per class
-   ≥ 150 annotated images total
-   Every class present in both train and validation sets

⚠️ If one class falls below 20 instances → **stop and reassess**, do NOT continue annotating blindly.

### 2.3.5. Annotation Stopping Criteria

Stop annotating when:
-   all classes reach ≥ 30 instances
-   new images add no new visual diversity
-   annotators start hesitating frequently (signal of saturation)

## 2.4. Scientific Motivation

Detecting concrete stage-related objects and structured crowd patterns in live metal concert imagery enables the study of object detection performance under extreme real-world conditions.

In particular, this task makes it possible to analyze:
-    model robustness under low-light and motion-degraded conditions,
-    confusion between visually related object classes (e.g. `guitar` vs `micro`, or `hands_raised` within `mosh_pit` regions),
-    the effect of object scale and visibility on detection performance,
-    the impact of crowd dynamics on localization accuracy.

This approach allows us to assess the limits of standard object detection models when applied outside their typical training distribution.

## 3. Dataset Construction

### 3.1. Data Sources

The dataset is composed of images collected from multiple sources:

-    Personal photographs taken during live metal concerts
-    Frames extracted from publicly available YouTube videos
-    Images collected from online image search engines

All data are used strictly for **academic and non-commercial purposes**.

```{text}
evolve/
├── notebooks/
│   └── evolve_detection.qmd
├── scripts/
│   └── youtube_pipeline.sh
├── data/
│   ├── youtube/
│   │   ├── videos/
│   │   └── frames/
│   ├── processed/
│   │   └── images/
│   └── annotations/
└── logs/
```


### 3.2. YouTube Scraping Pipeline

The YouTube data collection pipeline is implemented as an external shell script:
-   **Path**: `scripts/youtube_pipeline.sh`
-   **Purpose**:
    -   download publicly available YouTube videos using `yt-dlp`
    -   extract video frames at a fixed temporal rate using `ffmpeg`
    -   log all operations for reproducibility

The pipeline is kept **outside the notebook** in order to:
-   avoid accidental execution during notebook rendering,
-   ensure reproducibility via a single entry-point script,
-   allow execution on different machines or environments.

**Execution command (manual):**
```bash
bash scripts/youtube_pipeline.sh
```
Logs are written to:
-   `logs/ytdlp.log`
-   `logs/ffmpeg.log`

### 3.2.1. Queries

```{text}
# =========================================================
# Example YouTube search queries for EVOLVE dataset building
# =========================================================

# General live concert footage
metal concert live crowd
death metal concert live
metal concert night crowd
metal show live audience

# Smartphone / amateur recordings
metal concert smartphone
death metal live phone footage
metal concert handheld
metal concert fan cam

# Crowd dynamics (mosh pits, movement)
mosh pit metal concert
wall of death metal concert
circle pit metal live

# Festivals (high visual variability)
metal festival crowd night
metal open air festival night
metal festival live audience

# Full sets (long, consistent footage)
death metal full set live
metal live full concert
metal band full show live

# Front row / pit perspective
metal concert front row
metal live front row pit

# Recent footage (lighting + phone quality)
metal concert 2022 crowd
metal concert 2023 live
death metal live 2024

# Keywords to AVOID (too clean / professional)
official video
pro shot
hd live
4k concert
```

#### 3.2.2. YouTube Scraping Pipeline

The YouTube scraping pipeline is implemented as an external script:
-   Path: `scripts/youtube_pipeline.sh`
-   Executed manually from the command line
-   Kept outside notebooks to avoid accidental execution

Manual execution:
```bash
bash scripts/youtube_pipeline.sh
```

## 3.3. Scripts Overview

The project relies on a small set of external scripts to ensure modularity and reproducibility.

| Script | Location | Description |
|------|--------|------------|
| `youtube_pipeline.sh` | `scripts/` | Downloads YouTube videos and extracts frames |
| `preprocess_images.py` *(optional)* | `scripts/` | Applies light preprocessing for dataset inspection |
| `split_yolo_dataset.py` *(optional)* | `scripts/` | Splits annotated images into train / val / test sets |


Only lightweight inspection code is kept inside the workbook.
All heavy or destructive operations are delegated to scripts.

# 4. Preprocessing

### Important
The preprocessing described in this section is not used for **training**. It is performed exclusively for **exploratory analysis and dataset inspection**. All images used for training remain in their original resolution and format. YOLO’s internal preprocessing and augmentation mechanisms are used during training.

The following preprocessing steps are applied to homogenize the dataset:

-   resizing images to a fixed resolution
-   normalization of pixel values
-   optional contrast enhancement (CLAHE)
-   optional denoising

The preprocessing is intentionally light, in order to preserve the difficulty of the original scenes.

## 4.1. Preprocessing setup

```{python}
import os
from pathlib import Path
import cv2
import numpy as np
from tqdm import tqdm
```

## 4.2. Paths & parameters

```{python}
# Input / output directories
RAW_DIR = Path("../data/youtube/frames")
OUT_DIR = Path("../data/processed/images")

OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Target image size
IMG_SIZE = (224, 224)

# Preprocessing flags
APPLY_CLAHE = True
APPLY_DENOISING = False
```

## 4.3. Preprocessing functions

```{python}
def preprocess_image(img_path):
    """
    Preprocess a single image:
    - resize
    - optional denoising
    - contrast enhancement (CLAHE)
    - normalization
    """
    img = cv2.imread(str(img_path))
    img = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)

    # Resize
    img = cv2.resize(img, IMG_SIZE, interpolation=cv2.INTER_AREA)

    # Optional denoising
    if APPLY_DENOISING:
        img = cv2.fastNlMeansDenoisingColored(
            img, None, 5, 5, 7, 21
        )

    # Contrast enhancement (CLAHE on luminance)
    if APPLY_CLAHE:
        lab = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_RGB2LAB)
        l, a, b = cv2.split(lab)

        clahe = cv2.createCLAHE(clipLimit=2.0, tileGridSize=(8, 8))
        l = clahe.apply(l)

        lab = cv2.merge((l, a, b))
        img = cv2.cvtColor(lab, cv2.COLOR_LAB2RGB)

    # Normalize to [0, 1]
    img = img.astype(np.float32) / 255.0

    return img
```

## 4.4. Dataset preprocessing loop

```{python}
processed_count = 0

for root, _, files in os.walk(RAW_DIR):
    for file in tqdm(files):
        if not file.lower().endswith(".jpg"):
            continue

        src_path = Path(root) / file
        out_name = f"img_{processed_count:05d}.png"
        out_path = OUT_DIR / out_name

        img = preprocess_image(src_path)

        # Save image
        cv2.imwrite(
            str(out_path),
            cv2.cvtColor((img * 255).astype(np.uint8), cv2.COLOR_RGB2BGR)
        )

        processed_count += 1

processed_count
```

## 4.5. Sanity check

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import random

raw_imgs = list(RAW_DIR.rglob("*.jpg"))
proc_imgs = list(OUT_DIR.glob("*.png"))

raw_sample = random.choice(raw_imgs)
proc_sample = random.choice(proc_imgs)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4))

axes[0].imshow(cv2.cvtColor(cv2.imread(str(raw_sample)), cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
axes[0].set_title("Raw image")
axes[0].axis("off")

axes[1].imshow(cv2.cvtColor(cv2.imread(str(proc_sample)), cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
axes[1].set_title("Preprocessed image")
axes[1].axis("off")
```

Note: Preprocessing is applied exclusively for exploratory analysis and dataset inspection.  
Raw images are preserved for training the detection model in order to avoid unintended distribution shifts. 
All resizing, normalization, and augmentation steps used for training are handled internally by the YOLO pipeline.

# 5. Annotation Protocol

## 5.1. Annotation Tool

Annotations are performed using one of the following tools:
-   CVAT
-   Roboflow Annotate

Bounding boxes are drawn manually for each target class.

## 5.2. Annotation Guidelines

Annotations follow the **Ultra-Precise Annotation Protocol** defined in Section 2.3.

General principles:
-    Bounding boxes may be coarse (pixel-perfect precision is not required)
-    Multiple objects and multiple classes may appear in the same image
-    Overlapping bounding boxes are allowed
-    Small, ambiguous, or barely visible elements may be ignored

Only visually identifiable instances should be annotated.

## 5.3. Dataset Requirements

The dataset follows the project constraints:
-   6 classes
-   ≥ 30 annotated examples per class
-   ≥ 150 images total
-   split into training, validation, and test sets

# 6. Model Architecture

## 6.1. Chosen Model

A **YOLO-based object detection model** is selected for this project.

Motivations for this choice:
-   real-time object detection capability
-   strong performance on diverse visual conditions
-   availability of pre-trained weights
-   straightforward fine-tuning in PyTorch

### Dataset Architecture (YOLO)

```{text}
evolve/
├── notebooks/
│   ├── evolve_workbook.qmd        # Project definition, task, classes, annotation protocol
│   ├── evolve_training.ipynb      # YOLO training (GPU / Colab)
│   └── evolve_evaluation.ipynb    # Quantitative & qualitative evaluation
│
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   │   └── images/
│   │       ├── youtube/
│   │       │   ├── videos/        # Downloaded YouTube videos (.mp4)
│   │       │   └── frames/        # Extracted video frames (.jpg)
│   │       │
│   │       ├── personal/
│   │       │   └── photos/        # Personal concert photographs
│   │       │
│   │       └── web/
│   │           └── images/        # Images scraped from web search engines
│   │
│   ├── processed/
│   │   └── images/                # Preprocessed images (EDA / visualization only)
│   │
│   ├── annotations/
│   │   ├── raw/                   # CVAT / Roboflow exports (original format)
│   │   └── yolo/
│   │       ├── images/
│   │       │   └── all/            # Images selected for annotation
│   │       └── labels/
│   │           └── all/            # YOLO labels (before splitting)
│   │
│   └── yolo/
│       ├── images/
│       │   ├── train/              # Training images
│       │   ├── val/                # Validation images
│       │   └── test/               # Test images
│       │
│       ├── labels/
│       │   ├── train/              # Training labels
│       │   ├── val/                # Validation labels
│       │   └── test/               # Test labels
│       │
│       └── dataset.yaml            # YOLO dataset configuration
│
├── scripts/
│   ├── youtube_pipeline.sh         # YouTube scraping & frame extraction
│   ├── prepare_yolo_dataset.py    # Train/val/test split for YOLO
│   ├── count_instances.py          # Class instance statistics
│   ├── sanity_checks.py            # Image/label consistency checks
│   └── utils/
│       └── preprocess_images.py    # Optional preprocessing (EDA only)
│
├── runs/
│   └── detect/                     # YOLO training outputs (weights, metrics, predictions)
│
├── logs/
│   ├── ytdlp.log                   # yt-dlp logs
│   └── ffmpeg.log                  # ffmpeg logs
│
├── README.md
└── LICENSE
```

### `dataset.yaml`

Path: `data/yolo/dataset.yaml`

```yaml
path: ../data/yolo

train: images/train
val: images/val
test: images/test

nc: 6

names:
  0: amp
  1: guitar
  2: drums
  3: micro
  4: mosh_pit
  5: hands_raised
```

### Annotation format (YOLO)

Each image:
```
image_00012.jpg
image_00012.txt
```

Format of an annotation line:
```
<class_id> <x_center> <y_center> <width> <height>
```

Example (`image_00012.txt`)=
```{text}
0 0.51 0.78 0.32 0.18
3 0.62 0.44 0.08 0.15
5 0.48 0.85 0.60 0.20
```
→ `amp`, `micro`, `hands_raised`

### YOLOv8 train

```bash
pip install ultralytics
```
```bash
yolo detect train \
  model=yolov8n.pt \
  data=data/yolo/dataset.yaml \
  epochs=50 \
  imgsz=640 \
  batch=16 \
  device=auto
```

**Possible Models**:

| **Model**    | **Usage**                           |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| `yolov8n.pt` | fast / debug                        |
| `yolov8s.pt` | **good compromise**                 |
| `yolov8m.pt` | heavier, for a dataset > 300 images |



## 6.2. Transfer Learning Strategy

-   model initialized with pre-trained weights
-   detection head fine-tuned on the EVOLVE dataset
-   backbone optionally frozen during early epochs

# 7. Training Procedure

This section is implemented in a **separate training notebook**, as required by the project.

It includes:
-   dataset loading
-   hyperparameter selection
-   training curves
-   loss monitoring


# 8. Evaluation

Evaluation is performed in a **separate evaluation notebook**.

```bash
yolo detect val \
  model=runs/detect/train/weights/best.pt \
  data=data/yolo/dataset.yaml
```
Automatic results:
-   mAP@50
-   mAP@50:95
-   Precision / Recall per class

**Qualitative Inference (images)**

```bash
yolo detect predict \
  model=runs/detect/train/weights/best.pt \
  source=data/yolo/images/test \
  conf=0.25
```

## 8.1. Quantitative Metrics

-   mean Average Precision (mAP)
-   Intersection over Union (IoU)
-   precision / recall per class

## 8.2. Qualitative Analysis

-   visualization of correct detections
-   common failure cases
-   confusion between visually similar classes


# 9. Limitations & Ethical Considerations

-   Dataset size remains limited
-   Annotations are subjective and coarse
-   No redistribution of collected images
-   Academic and non-commercial usage only

<details><summary><str>TO DO LIST</str><summary>

1. Récupérer des images
2. Geler les conventions
    -   format image unique (`.jpg`)
    -   renommer les images (`img_000001.jpg`, `img_000002.jpg`, etc.)
    -   vérifier qu'on a bien les mêmes 6 classes et le même ordre (celui de `dataset.yaml`)
3. Centraliser les images candidates
    -   tout mettre dans `data/raw/`
    -   retirer les images inutilisables (trop floyes, trop sombres, sujet invisible, etc.)
    -   faire un dédup rapide (retirer les frames trop similaires)
    -   **output attendu** : `data/raw/`contient uniquement des images "annotables"
4. Créer le pool d'annotation
    -   copier (pas déplacer) les images retenus dans `data/annotations/yolo/images/`
    -   renommer si nécessaire en séquence propre (flemme)
    -   **output attendu** : `data/annotations/yolo/images/`= les images qui seront annotées
5. Annotation (CVAT/Roboflow)
    -   annoter dans l'outil les 6 classes
    -   exporter en YOLO format
    -   récupérer les images + les labels `.txt`
    -   mettre les exports finaux dans :
        -   images : `data/annotations/yolo/images/ (si elles y sont déjà, vérifier la cohérence des noms)
        -   labels : `data/annotations/yolo/labels/`
    - **output attendu** : pour chaque `img_xxxxxx.jpg`il existe `img_xxxxxx.txt`
6. Sanity checks avant split
    -   lancer le script `scripts/sanity_checks.py`
    -   corriger :
        -   les images sans label
        -   les labels sans image
        -   les erreurs évidentes de classe (ex.: id hors [0.5])
    -   lancer le script `scripts/count_instances.py`
        -   vérifier la balance (minimum syndical = ~30 instances par classe)
    - **output attendu** : dataset cohérent + stats d'instances
7. Split + création du dataset YOLO final
    -   lancer `scripts/prepare_yolo_dataset.py`
    -   refaire un sanity check (oui, encore) : lancer `scripts/sanity_checks.py`
    -   **output attendu** :
        -   `data/yolo/images/train|val|test`
        -   `data/yolo/labels/train|val|test`
8. Vérifier `dataset.yaml`
    -   ouvrir `data/yolo/dataset.yaml`
    -   vérifier que `path:` et les chemins `train/val/test/ sont cohérents depuis l'endroit où est lancé YOLO
    -   **output attendu** : YOLO trouve correctement les images + labels
9. Entraînement (Colab/GPU)
    -   uploader `data/yolo/`+ notebooks Jupyter + `dataset.yaml` sur Colab
    -   lancer `evolve_notebook.ipynb`(sanity/debug)
    -    puis un vrai run avec `yolov8s`
    -   **output attendu** : `runs/evolve_yolo/weights/best.pt`
10. Évaluation et figures
    -   lancer `evolve_evaluation.ipynb`
    -   récupérer :
        -   per-class AP
        -   mAP global
        -   6-12 images "best examples/worst failures" avec commentaires
    -   **outputs attendus** : graphiques + exemples + inteprétation
11. Loop d'amélioration (si résultats décevants)
    -   rééquilibrer les classes (+ d'exemples des classes faibles)
    -   ajouter de la diversité (angles, distances, luminosité)
    -   ajuster hyperparams (epochs, imgsz, batch, augment)
    -   tester `yolov8m` (si dataset assez grand)

</details>
